sources.notes: Statement and constraints: construct the target point with bounded triangle operations.
Official editorial confirms the parallelogram-ladder construction and angle-case proof.
Luogu mirror used as a secondary statement check; no separate Luogu solution was used as evidence.
Independent Chinese writeup used to cross-check the angle adjustment and short-final-segment cases.

annotation.summary: Construct a sequence of triangle additions that creates target point C=(p,q) from initial points A=(0,0), B=(1,0).
annotation.constraints: All triangle side lengths must stay in [0.5,1], and the number of operations is at most ceil(2*sqrt(p^2+q^2)).
annotation.core_idea: Advance an existing edge as the front of a strip. Two triangle additions form one parallelogram step, giving nearly one unit of progress while preserving legal side lengths when the direction angle lies in a safe 30-to-60 degree range. If the direct direction is outside the safe range, first add a helper triangle to rotate the usable base edge, then run the ladder and handle the final short segment by explicit cases.
annotation.complexity: O(m) time and output where m is the operation bound
tricks: Use two triangles as one parallelogram step.
Prove side-length legality with angle/cosine-law bounds.
Rotate the initial edge into a safe angle range.
Handle the final short segment separately to preserve the exact operation bound.

solution_variants.name: angle-case-parallelogram-ladder
solution_variants.summary: Choose a safe-angle strip, repeatedly add parallelogram cells by two triangles, and finish with a short-segment case construction.

tags.evidence: Correctness depends on Euclidean angle ranges, cosine-law distance bounds, and geometric construction of triangle chains.
The task requires explicitly outputting coordinates and existing-edge choices for every triangle operation.
The construction splits by safe-angle availability and final segment length.
The solution must carefully output floating-point coordinates and edge references within precision tolerances.
